grammar.usage: Used as nouns, after prepositions, or after certain verbs (e.g. admit, avoid, enjoy).
Used after adjectives, to express purpose, or after certain verbs (e.g. decide, hope, manage).
grammar.example: Your answer appears to be wrong.

grammar.usage: Used to express preference or give advice.
grammar.example: I prefer reading to writing. / S + would rather + than... / You'd better ask.

grammar.usage: Used to express the purpose of an action.
grammar.example: I went to university to avoid getting a job!

blocks.content: 1. Verbs followed by -ing (normally with or without an object)

rows: to agree that something is true, especially unwillingly
to speak about something quickly, giving little detail
to recognize the full value of something
to be annoyed or worried by something
to prevent something from happening or to keep away from something
to fail to hit, reach, catch, or meet
to be unable to resist doing something
to delay something until a later time
to do something repeatedly to improve skill
to delay doing something until a later date
to get free from something or to avoid something
to withstand the action or effect of
to confront or deal with a difficult situation
to expose to danger or loss
to want to have or do something
to mention or imply as a possibility
to have a desire to do something
to perceive the intended meaning of
to bring to an end; complete
to include or affect someone or something

blocks.content: 2. Verbs followed by full infinitive (to + Vbare)

rows: having the power, skill, means, or opportunity to do something
to want something to happen and think that it is possible
to have enough money or time for
to succeed in doing or dealing with something, especially something difficult
to present or proffer something for someone to accept or reject
to seem; give the impression of being
to decide on and make arrangements for in advance
to say something in order to obtain information or request someone to do something
to make ready for use or consideration
to try to do or achieve something
to behave so as to make it appear that something is the case when in fact it is not
to ask for something in an anxious or humble way
to assure someone that one will definitely do something
to select from a number of possibilities
to indicate that one is not willing to do something
to make a choice or judgment
to give the impression of being
to give support, confidence, or hope to
to regularly or frequently behave in a particular way
to regard something as likely to happen
to have a desire to possess or do something
to be unsuccessful in achieving one's goal
to feel or express a strong desire or hope for something
used to say politely that you want something
to make it easier for someone to do something

blocks.content: 3. Verbs followed by Object + Vbare or Object + V-ing

bullets:
**S + Verb + Object + Vbare**: to describe a completed action (from start to finish).
* Verbs: **feel, hear, watch, notice, see, let, make**
EX: I heard Mrs. Jenkins tell Julie not to do that. (I heard the whole conversation.)

**S + Verb + Object + V-ing**: to describe an action in progress, or only a part of the action.
* Verbs: **feel, hear, see, notice, watch**
EX: I heard Miss Jenkins telling Julie not to do that. (I heard only part of it; it was in progress.)

**Note on passive forms:** When **hear, make, see** are used in the passive, they are followed by a full infinitive (to + Vbare).
EX: The coach made us train hard. → We were made to train hard.

blocks.content: 4. Verbs followed by to-infinitive or V-ing (No change in meaning)

bullets: Verbs: **begin, can't bear/stand, continue, hate, intend, love, prefer, start**
EX: We continued to do experiments in the lab all afternoon.
EX: We continued doing experiments in the lab all afternoon.

blocks.content: 5. Verbs followed by to-infinitive or V-ing (Change in meaning)

bullets: Verbs: **consider, forget, go on, imagine, learn, like, regret, remember, stop, teach, try, mean**

**remember + V-ing**: recall a past action / memory.
EX: I remember teachers at my school hitting children when they were naughty!

**remember + to-infinitive**: remember to perform a future or planned action.
EX: Did you remember to do your homework?
*(Note: For differences in meaning of other verbs, see the grammar reference at the back of the book)*

richGrammar.title: B) PREFER, WOULD RATHER, HAD BETTER

bullets:
**General preference (prefer + N/V-ing + to + N/V-ing)**: used to describe general preferences.
EX: I prefer Biology to History.
EX: I prefer reading English texts to speaking in English.

**Specific preference (would prefer + to + Vbare + rather than + Vbare / to + Vbare)**: used to describe a preference in a specific situation.
EX: I'd prefer to have the lesson on Thursday rather than ((to) have it) on Friday, if that's possible.

blocks.intro: We use would rather to express preference.

items.label: a) With one subject (preference of the speaker):
bullets:
**Present preference (S + would rather (+ not) + Vbare + than + Vbare)**
EX: I'd rather have the lesson on Wednesday than (have it) on Tuesday, if that's possible.
EX: We would rather not travel to Korea.

**Past preference (S + would rather (+ not) + have + V3/ed)**
EX: I would rather have bought a shirt than a dress.
EX: I would rather not have bought this shirt.

items.label: b) With two subjects (desire for someone else's action):
bullets:
**Present desire (S1 + would rather (that) + S2 + Vbare)**: used to express what someone wants another person to do in the present.
EX: I would rather that you come back to me.
EX: I would rather that you not come back to me.

**Present hypothetical desire (S1 + would rather (that) + S2 + V2/ed / weren't)**: used to express a wish that is contrary to facts in the present. Note that 'be' is always 'were/weren't' for all subjects.
EX: Henry would rather that his girlfriend worked in the same department as he does.
EX: Henry would rather that Jenny didn't work in the same department as he does.
EX: Tom would rather that he weren't a teacher.

**Past hypothetical desire (S1 + would rather (that) + S2 + had + V3/ed)**: used to express a wish that is contrary to facts in the past.
EX: Bob would rather that Jill had gone to class yesterday.
EX: Bob would rather that Jill hadn't gone to class yesterday.

**had better + Vbare**: used to give advice to someone in a specific situation.
EX: You'd better ask your parents if you can come on the school trip.

blocks.content: Ways to express the purpose of an action:

bullets:
**1. to + Vbare**
EX: I went to university to avoid getting a job!
* **Warning:** Do not use 'not + to-infinitive' for negative purpose.
✗ I went to university not to get a job!

**2. in order + to + Vbare**
EX: I went to university in order to avoid getting a job!

**3. so as + to + Vbare**
EX: I went to university so as to avoid getting a job!

**4. so (that) + could + Vbare**
EX: I went to university so (that) I could avoid getting a job!

vocabulary.meaning: a space or room just below the roof of a house
vocabulary.example: We store our old suitcases in the attic.

vocabulary.meaning: an extra bedroom in a house that is not used regularly
vocabulary.example: Our guests can sleep in the spare bedroom.

vocabulary.meaning: withstand the action or effect of; or refrain from doing or having something tempting
vocabulary.example: I couldn't resist eating the last slice of cake.

vocabulary.meaning: give support, confidence, or hope to someone
vocabulary.example: My parents always encourage me to try my best.

vocabulary.meaning: a watch that has been owned or used by someone else before
vocabulary.example: He bought a second-hand watch at the flea market.
I detest having to wake up early on weekends.

vocabulary.meaning: used to convey that what is asserted is very likely though not known for certain
vocabulary.example: Presumably, they will arrive before dinner.

vocabulary.meaning: have an urge or inclination to do something, especially something wrong or unwise
vocabulary.example: I was tempted to buy the expensive shoes.

vocabulary.meaning: lightly touched to cause a tingling sensation and laughter
vocabulary.example: The baby laughed when he was tickled on his tummy.

vocabulary.meaning: tricks played on someone in order to make them look foolish and to amuse others
vocabulary.example: He is always playing practical jokes on his colleagues.

vocabulary.meaning: feeling less eager to work or make an effort
vocabulary.example: She felt demotivated by the lack of appreciation.

vocabulary.meaning: come near or nearer to someone or something in distance or time
vocabulary.example: The train slowed down as it approached the station.
